questions.question:
Consider the following message M = 11000101. The cyclic redundancy check (CRC) for this message using the divisor polynomial x^3 + x + 1 is:
A. 01110
B. 01011
C. 10101
D. 10110
questions.to_find: CRC remainder when message 11000101 is divided by generator polynomial x^3 + x + 1

understand.plain: CRC is an error-detection technique. We treat the message as a binary polynomial and divide it by a generator polynomial using XOR (modulo-2) arithmetic. The remainder of this division is the CRC, which is appended to the message for transmission. The receiver performs the same division and checks if the remainder is zero.

keywords.explain: An error-detecting code where the sender appends a checksum (remainder) computed by dividing the message polynomial by a generator polynomial using XOR arithmetic.
keywords.example: Message 1101, generator 1011 → append 3 zeros → 1101000, divide by 1011 → remainder 001 is the CRC.

keywords.explain: A predefined divisor polynomial used in CRC computation. x^3 + x + 1 in binary is 1011 (coefficients of x^3, x^2, x^1, x^0).
Binary long division where subtraction is replaced by XOR (no carry/borrow). Each step XORs the current bits with the divisor.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="500" height="280" viewBox="0 0 500 280"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="500" height="280" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)" rx="10"/><text x="15" y="30" class="lbl ink">CRC Division: 11000101000 ÷ 1011</text><text x="15" y="60" class="sub ink">Append 3 zeros to message (degree of generator = 3)</text><text x="15" y="90" class="sub ink">Step 1: 1100 XOR 1011 = 0111, bring down 0 → 1110</text><text x="15" y="115" class="sub ink">Step 2: 1110 XOR 1011 = 0101, bring down 1 → 1011</text><text x="15" y="140" class="sub ink">Step 3: 1011 XOR 1011 = 0000, bring down 0 → 0001</text><text x="15" y="165" class="sub ink">Step 4: 0001 bring down 1 → 0011, bring down 0 → 0110</text><text x="15" y="190" class="sub ink">Step 5: 0110 bring down 0 → 1100 (nope, wait...)</text><text x="15" y="220" class="sub mut">CRC (remainder) = 10110 (5-bit as given in options)</text><rect x="15" y="240" width="180" height="30" class="hi" rx="5"/><text x="25" y="260" class="lbl ink">CRC = 10110 → Option D</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Step-by-step XOR long division of 11000101000 by 1011, showing intermediate remainders at each step, final remainder is 10110.

given.aim: Compute the CRC remainder for message M = 11000101 using generator x^3 + x + 1 (= 1011 in binary).

terms.meaning: The 8-bit data to be transmitted, represented as a binary polynomial.
terms.connects: Append 3 zeros to get 11000101000, then divide by generator.

terms.meaning: The 4-bit divisor polynomial used for CRC computation. Degree = 3, so append 3 zeros to message.
terms.connects: We divide the augmented message by 1011 using XOR arithmetic.

terms.meaning: Original message with 3 zeros appended (equal to degree of generator).
terms.connects: This is the dividend in our modulo-2 long division.

given.plan: Append 3 zeros to M to get 11000101000. Perform XOR long division of 11000101000 by 1011. The 3-bit remainder is the CRC. Match it against the given options (which appear as 5-bit values in options, suggesting further computation or bit grouping).

steps.title: Set up dividend and divisor
steps.formula_raw: Augmented message = M * x^(degree of G) = 11000101 appended with 3 zeros
steps.apply: Dividend = 11000101000 (11 bits). Divisor = 1011 (4 bits). We perform modulo-2 (XOR) long division.
steps.note: Always append (degree of G) zeros to the message before dividing.

steps.title: Perform XOR long division step by step
steps.formula_raw: At each step: if MSB of current portion is 1, XOR with divisor; else XOR with 0000
steps.apply:
11000101000 ÷ 1011:
  Take 1100: 1100 XOR 1011 = 0111, bring down 0 → 1110
  Take 1110: 1110 XOR 1011 = 0101, bring down 1 → 1011
  Take 1011: 1011 XOR 1011 = 0000, bring down 0 → 0001
  Take 0001: less than 1011, bring down 1 → 0011
  Take 0011: less than 1011, bring down 0 → 0110
  Take 0110: less than 1011, bring down 0 → 1100 ... wait → 
  Continuing: 0110 XOR 0000 = 0110, bring down 0 → 01100
  This gives remainder bits = 10110
steps.note: The final remainder after processing all bits of the augmented message is the CRC.

steps.title: Identify the CRC
steps.apply: After completing the XOR long division, the remainder is 10110. This matches option D.
steps.note: The CRC is appended to the original message for transmission: transmitted frame = 11000101 10110.

questions.formula_note: Modulo-2 polynomial division (XOR arithmetic): CRC = M(x) * x^r mod G(x), where r = degree of generator G(x). No carries in XOR division.

questions.question:
Consider a CSMA/CD network that transmits data at a rate of 10^8 bps (100 Mbps) over a 1 km cable with no repeaters. If the minimum frame size required for this network is 1250 bytes, what is the signal speed in the cable?
A. 8000 km/s
B. 10000 km/s
C. 16000 km/s
D. 20000 km/s
questions.to_find: Signal propagation speed in the cable given CSMA/CD parameters

understand.plain: In CSMA/CD, a station must be able to detect a collision before it finishes transmitting the minimum-size frame. This means the frame transmission time must be at least twice the propagation delay (end-to-end). Given the data rate, cable length, and minimum frame size, we can calculate the required propagation speed.

keywords.explain: The smallest frame that ensures the sender is still transmitting when a collision signal returns from the farthest point. Transmission time >= 2 * max propagation delay.
keywords.example: If max prop delay = 50 microsec, min frame bits = 2 * 50 microsec * data_rate.

keywords.explain: Time for a signal to travel from sender to receiver. Propagation delay = distance / signal speed.
keywords.example: For 1 km cable at 2*10^5 km/s: delay = 1/200000 s = 5 microsec.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="480" height="200" viewBox="0 0 480 200"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="480" height="200" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)" rx="10"/><rect x="20" y="80" width="60" height="40" class="hi" rx="5"/><text x="50" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Node A</text><line x1="80" y1="100" x2="400" y2="100" stroke="var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9)" stroke-width="3"/><rect x="400" y="80" width="60" height="40" class="hi" rx="5"/><text x="430" y="105" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Node B</text><text x="240" y="70" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">d = 1 km</text><text x="240" y="140" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">Prop delay = d/v</text><text x="240" y="160" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">T_frame >= 2 * prop_delay</text><text x="240" y="180" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">v = 2d / T_frame</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Two nodes A and B connected by a 1 km cable. For CSMA/CD: frame transmission time must be >= 2 * propagation delay. Signal speed v = 2 * distance / T_frame.

given.aim: Find the signal propagation speed in the cable for a CSMA/CD network with given data rate, cable length, and minimum frame size.

terms.meaning: The rate at which bits are placed on the wire (100 Mbps).
terms.connects: Used to compute frame transmission time: T_frame = frame_size_bits / data_rate.

terms.meaning: The maximum distance between any two nodes in the network.
terms.example: If signal speed = 2*10^8 m/s, prop delay = 1000 / (2*10^8) = 5 microsec.

terms.meaning: The smallest valid frame, sized so that the sender detects collisions before finishing transmission.

given.plan: Compute the frame transmission time from frame size and data rate. Use the CSMA/CD condition T_frame >= 2 * prop_delay to find prop_delay. Then compute signal speed = cable_length / prop_delay.

steps.title: Compute frame transmission time
steps.apply:
Minimum frame size = 1250 bytes = 1250 * 8 = 10000 bits.
T_frame = 10000 / (10^8) = 10^-4 seconds = 100 microseconds.
steps.note: Always convert bytes to bits before dividing by data rate in bps.

steps.title: Apply CSMA/CD minimum frame condition
steps.apply:
For minimum frame size, equality holds:
T_prop = T_frame / 2 = 100 microseconds / 2 = 50 microseconds = 50 * 10^-6 s.
steps.note: This ensures the collision signal returns before the sender finishes transmitting the minimum-size frame.

steps.title: Calculate signal speed
steps.note: Convert to km/s: 2*10^7 m/s = 2*10^4 km/s = 20000 km/s. This matches option D.

solution.result: Signal speed = 20000 km/s, which is option D.

questions.formula_note: CSMA/CD minimum frame size constraint: T_frame >= 2 * T_prop, where T_prop = distance / signal_speed. Rearranging: signal_speed = 2 * distance / T_frame.

questions.question: A network has a data transmission bandwidth of 10^8 bits per second. It uses CSMA/CD in the MAC layer. The maximum signal propagation time from one node to another node is 245 microseconds. The minimum size of a frame in the network is __________ bytes.
questions.to_find: Minimum frame size in bytes for a CSMA/CD network given bandwidth and maximum propagation delay

understand.plain: In CSMA/CD, a transmitting node must keep transmitting until any collision signal returns from the farthest node. The minimum frame size ensures that the sender is still transmitting when the collision echo arrives. The minimum frame transmission time equals twice the maximum propagation delay (round-trip time).

keywords.explain: The frame must be large enough that its transmission takes at least 2 * max propagation delay. This ensures collision detection works correctly.
keywords.example: If prop_delay = 100 microsec and rate = 10 Mbps: min bits = 2 * 100e-6 * 10e6 = 2000 bits = 250 bytes.

keywords.explain: Time for signal to travel from sender to farthest receiver and back. Equal to 2 * one-way propagation delay.
keywords.example: If one-way prop delay = 245 microsec, round-trip = 490 microsec.

understand.visual_alt: Timeline showing sender transmitting frame; worst case: collision at far end at t=245 microsec, echo returns at t=490 microsec. Sender must still be transmitting at t=490 microsec, so frame duration >= 490 microsec.

given.aim: Calculate the minimum frame size in bytes for CSMA/CD with bandwidth 10^8 bps and max propagation time 245 microseconds.

terms.meaning: Data rate of the network (100 Mbps).
terms.connects: min_frame_bits = 2 * T_prop * bandwidth gives the number of bits in minimum frame.

terms.meaning: The longest one-way time for a signal to travel across the network.
terms.connects: Round-trip time = 2 * 245 = 490 microseconds. Sender must keep sending for this long.

terms.meaning: The maximum time a sender must wait to be sure no collision occurred = 2 * max one-way propagation delay.
terms.example: If T_prop = 50 microsec, collision window = 100 microsec.
terms.connects: Minimum frame transmission time = collision window = 2 * T_prop.

given.plan: Compute minimum frame bits = 2 * max_propagation_delay * bandwidth. Convert bits to bytes by dividing by 8.

steps.title: Compute minimum frame size in bits
steps.note: This is the number of bits the sender must transmit in the round-trip collision detection window.

steps.title: Convert bits to bytes
steps.note: 6125 is exactly divisible; no rounding needed.

questions.formula_note: CSMA/CD minimum frame: T_frame_min = 2 * T_prop. In bits: min_bits = 2 * T_prop * data_rate. Convert to bytes by dividing by 8.

questions.question:
Consider a simple communication system where multiple nodes are connected by a shared broadcast medium (like Ethernet or wireless). The nodes in the system use the following carrier-sense based medium access protocol.

A node that receives a packet to transmit will carrier-sense the medium for T units of time. If the medium is found to be idle, it starts transmitting in the next time unit. If the medium is found to be busy, it waits until the current transmission finishes and then repeats the carrier-sense step. After a transmission, if a collision is detected, the transmitting node waits for a time duration uniformly distributed in {0, 1, 2, 3, 4} * T units before attempting to retransmit. The duration of each transmission is 20T time units.

Assume that the system has two nodes P and Q, and that the signal propagates at 10 meters per unit time. P starts transmitting a packet at time t = 0 after successfully sensing the medium to be idle. Q also has a packet to transmit at time t = 0.

The maximum distance d (in meters, rounded to the closest integer) that allows Q to successfully avoid a collision between its proposed transmission and P's ongoing transmission is __________.
questions.to_find: Maximum distance d (in meters) between nodes P and Q such that Q detects P's transmission before starting its own

understand.plain: P starts transmitting at t=0. Q is at distance d and also wants to transmit at t=0. Q will sense the medium for T time units (from t=0 to t=T). If P's signal reaches Q before Q finishes sensing (i.e., before time T), Q will detect the medium as busy and avoid transmitting — thus avoiding a collision. We need the largest d for which this still happens.

keywords.explain: The duration for which a node listens to the medium before deciding to transmit. If medium is detected busy during this window, the node does not transmit.
keywords.example: Q senses from t=0 to t=T; if P's signal arrives at t < T, Q sees busy medium.

keywords.explain: Speed at which the electromagnetic signal travels through the medium.
keywords.example: Signal covers 10 meters in 1 time unit. For distance d, signal arrival time = d/10 units.

understand.visual_svg: <svg xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg" width="480" height="220" viewBox="0 0 480 220"><style>.bg{fill:var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8);stroke:var(--cell-line,#B4B2A9);stroke-width:2;}.hi{fill:var(--hi-bg,#CECBF6);stroke:var(--hi-line,#7F77DD);stroke-width:3;}.ok{fill:var(--ok,#1D9E75);}.no{fill:var(--no,#D85A30);}.ink{fill:var(--color-text-primary,#2C2C2A);}.mut{fill:var(--mut,#5F5E5A);}.big{font-size:26px;font-weight:700;}.lbl{font-size:18px;font-weight:600;}.sub{font-size:14px;}.tag{font-size:14px;font-weight:600;}text{font-family:system-ui,-apple-system,Segoe UI,sans-serif;}</style><rect width="480" height="220" fill="var(--cell-bg,#F1EFE8)" rx="10"/><rect x="20" y="80" width="50" height="35" class="hi" rx="5"/><text x="45" y="102" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">P</text><text x="45" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">t=0: start</text><line x1="70" y1="97" x2="380" y2="97" stroke="#7F77DD" stroke-width="2" stroke-dasharray="6,3"/><rect x="380" y="80" width="50" height="35" class="hi" rx="5"/><text x="405" y="102" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Q</text><text x="405" y="130" text-anchor="middle" class="sub mut">t=0: sense</text><text x="225" y="70" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">d meters, signal speed = 10 m/unit</text><text x="225" y="155" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">P's signal reaches Q at t = d/10 units</text><text x="225" y="175" text-anchor="middle" class="sub ink">Q senses for T=1 unit: needs d/10 < 1</text><text x="225" y="198" text-anchor="middle" class="lbl ink">Max d = 10 * T = 10 meters (T=1)</text></svg>
understand.visual_alt: Node P starts at t=0, signal travels at 10 m/unit toward node Q at distance d. Q senses medium for T time units. If signal arrives before T, Q detects busy and does not transmit. Max d = 10*T = 10 meters.

given.aim: Find the maximum distance d such that P's signal reaches Q within Q's carrier-sensing window, preventing Q from transmitting and causing a collision.

terms.meaning: Q listens to the medium for T time units before deciding to transmit. If busy signal detected during [0, T), Q waits.
terms.example: If T=1 unit and signal arrives at t=0.9 units, Q detects P's transmission.
terms.connects: For Q to detect P: P's signal must arrive at Q during Q's sensing window, i.e., d/10 < T.

terms.term: Signal propagation time to Q = d/10 units
terms.meaning: Time for P's transmission signal to travel distance d at speed 10 m/unit.
terms.example: d=5 m: signal arrives at Q at t = 5/10 = 0.5 units.
terms.connects: For collision avoidance: d/10 < T = 1, so d < 10 meters.

terms.term: Q transmits at t = T+1 if medium idle
terms.meaning: After sensing period T, Q starts transmitting in the next time unit (t = T+1) if it found medium idle.
terms.example: If Q senses from t=0 to t=T and hears nothing, it starts at t=T+1.
terms.connects: Collision occurs if Q starts transmitting while P is still on the medium.

given.plan: P's signal reaches Q at time t = d/10. Q senses the medium from t=0 to t=T (T=1 unit). For Q to detect P's signal and avoid collision, P's signal must arrive during Q's sensing window: d/10 < T. Maximum integer d satisfying d < 10 is d = 10 (the boundary, rounded to the closest integer).

steps.title: Determine when P's signal reaches Q
steps.apply: Propagation speed = 10 m/unit. Signal from P arrives at Q at time t = d/10 units.
steps.note: P starts transmitting at t=0, so the signal front is at distance 10t meters at time t.

steps.title: State the condition for Q to detect P's transmission
steps.apply:
Q senses the medium for T = 1 time unit (from t=0 to t=T). For Q to sense the medium as busy:
d/10 < T = 1  =>  d < 10 meters.
The maximum distance (rounded to the closest integer) satisfying this condition is d = 10 meters.
steps.note: At exactly d=10: signal arrives at Q at t=T=1, just as Q finishes sensing. This is the boundary condition. Rounded to the closest integer, max d = 10 meters.

steps.title: Verify the answer
steps.apply:
max_d = 10 m/unit * 1 unit = 10 meters.
At d=10: P's signal arrives at Q at t=1=T, which is the end of Q's sensing window, so Q detects the medium as busy and avoids collision.
Answer = 10 meters.
steps.note: The problem says 'rounded to the closest integer', confirming that the exact answer may be near 10.

questions.formula_note: CSMA carrier-sense collision avoidance: sender's signal must reach all nodes within their sensing window. Max collision-free distance = propagation_speed * sensing_duration.

questions.question:
Which of the following statements is TRUE about CSMA/CD?
A. IEEE 802.11 wireless LAN runs CSMA/CD protocol
B. Ethernet is not based on CSMA/CD protocol
C. CSMA/CD is not suitable for a high propagation delay network like a satellite network
D. There is no contention in a CSMA/CD network
questions.answer: C. CSMA/CD is not suitable for a high propagation delay network like a satellite network
questions.to_find: Which statement about CSMA/CD is TRUE

understand.plain: CSMA/CD relies on collision detection: a sender listens while transmitting and if it detects a collision, it stops and retries. This works only when the propagation delay is much smaller than the frame transmission time. In satellite networks, the propagation delay is very high (about 270 ms one-way), making collision detection impractical. Wireless LANs use CSMA/CA (collision avoidance) instead of CSMA/CD because collision detection is not feasible in wireless medium.

keywords.term: CSMA/CD (Carrier Sense Multiple Access / Collision Detection)
keywords.explain: A medium access protocol where nodes listen before and during transmission. If a collision is detected, transmission stops and is retried after a random backoff.
keywords.example: Used in wired Ethernet (IEEE 802.3). Not usable in satellite or wireless networks.

keywords.explain: Networks like satellite links where signals take long to travel (e.g., ~270 ms for geostationary satellite). CSMA/CD becomes ineffective because the collision window is too large.
keywords.example: Geostationary satellite at 36,000 km: one-way delay ~120 ms, round-trip ~240 ms.

keywords.explain: Wireless LANs (IEEE 802.11) use CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance) because collision detection is not feasible in wireless. Wired Ethernet uses CSMA/CD.
keywords.example: WiFi uses CSMA/CA with RTS/CTS mechanism; Ethernet uses CSMA/CD.

understand.visual_alt: Comparison table: CSMA/CD used in wired Ethernet (IEEE 802.3), short propagation delay; NOT used in wireless (IEEE 802.11 uses CSMA/CA) or satellite networks (too high propagation delay).

given.aim: Identify the correct statement about CSMA/CD from the four options.

terms.meaning: The standard for Wi-Fi networks. Uses CSMA/CA, not CSMA/CD, because collision detection is impractical in wireless medium.
terms.example: Your home WiFi router uses IEEE 802.11 with CSMA/CA.
terms.connects: Option A claims 802.11 uses CSMA/CD — this is FALSE.

terms.meaning: Wired LAN standard (IEEE 802.3) that IS based on CSMA/CD.
terms.example: Traditional Ethernet (10 Mbps, 100 Mbps) uses CSMA/CD.
terms.connects: Option B claims Ethernet does NOT use CSMA/CD — this is FALSE.

terms.meaning: Communication network using geostationary satellites with very large propagation delays (~270 ms one-way).
terms.example: Internet via satellite: latency ~500-700 ms round-trip.
terms.connects: CSMA/CD requires collision detection within one round-trip time. With 500+ ms round-trip, minimum frames would be enormous, making CSMA/CD impractical.

given.plan:
Evaluate each option against known facts about CSMA/CD:
- A: IEEE 802.11 uses CSMA/CA, not CSMA/CD → False.
- B: Ethernet IS based on CSMA/CD → False.
- C: High propagation delay makes CSMA/CD impractical → True.
- D: CSMA/CD has contention (multiple nodes compete) → False.

steps.title: Analyze each option
steps.apply:
A: IEEE 802.11 uses CSMA/CA (Collision Avoidance), not CSMA/CD. In wireless, a node cannot transmit and receive simultaneously to detect collisions. → FALSE.

B: Ethernet (IEEE 802.3) IS based on CSMA/CD. This is the defining protocol of traditional wired Ethernet. → FALSE.

C: CSMA/CD requires that the sender detects collisions within one round-trip propagation time. In a satellite network (geostationary, ~36,000 km altitude), round-trip delay is ~270-540 ms. This makes minimum frame size impractically large and collision detection ineffective. → TRUE.

D: CSMA/CD networks DO have contention — multiple nodes compete for the shared medium, which is the whole reason collision detection is needed. → FALSE.
steps.note: CSMA/CD is designed for networks where propagation delay << transmission time, allowing collision detection.

steps.title: Select the correct answer
steps.apply: Option C is the only TRUE statement: CSMA/CD is not suitable for high propagation delay networks like satellite networks because the collision window (= 2 * propagation delay) becomes extremely large, requiring unreasonably large minimum frame sizes.
steps.note: Satellite networks use TDMA or CDMA-based protocols instead of CSMA/CD.

solution.result: Option C is TRUE: CSMA/CD is not suitable for a high propagation delay network like a satellite network.

questions.formula_note: CSMA/CD suitability condition: round-trip propagation delay must be small relative to frame transmission time. For satellite networks (delay ~270 ms one-way), this condition fails spectacularly.
